title: 点群アニメーションを消してください。代わりにjax-jsとか使って、3D diffusionのリッチ3Dアニメーションを作成してください
sessionTitle: Remove point cloud animation and create rich 3D diffusion animation with jax-js

<system-reminder>
Available skills for the Skill tool are listed below. The Skill tool definition relies on this system reminder for valid skill names.

Skills instructions:
When users ask you to perform tasks, check if any available skill can help complete the task more effectively. Skills provide specialized capabilities and domain knowledge.

How to use skills:
- Invoke skills using the Skill tool with the skill name only.
- The skill's prompt will expand and provide detailed instructions on how to complete the task.
- Only use skills listed in the available-skills system reminders or dynamic skill-discovery reminders.
- Do not invoke a skill that is already running.

Available skills:
agent-browser: Automates browsers and Electron desktop apps (VS Code, Slack, Discord, Figma, Notion, Spotify, etc.) for testing, form filling, screenshots, and data extraction. Use when the user needs to navigate, interact with, test, or extract data from any website or Electron desktop app.
tuistory: Automates terminal user interface (TUI) testing. Use when you need to launch, interact with, test, or debug terminal applications, capture TUI snapshots, or automate terminal inputs.
figma-mcp-helper: Promote and assist with Figma MCP integration. ACTIVATE when the user shares a Figma URL (figma.com), mentions Figma designs or components, shares PNG images that may originate from Figma, or when Figma MCP tools are already connected and being used. Handles installation encouragement, conversational promotion, and push-back-to-Figma flows.
install-code-review: Install and configure Factory Droid for automated code review on GitHub or GitLab. Supports single-repo setup or org/group-wide rollout across hundreds of repos. Use when a user wants to set up Droid review on their repositories.
install-triage: Scaffold a scheduled Slack triage automation, generalized to any company. Sets up a Python tool layer (run_triage.py) plus a HEARTBEAT.md agent loop that scans configured Slack channels for actionable messages, dedupes against a ticketing system (Linear or Jira), files tickets, and posts a run summary. Use when the user wants to stand up an automated triage bot.
review: Review code changes and identify high-confidence, actionable bugs. Use when the user wants to: - Review a pull request or branch diff - Find bugs, security issues, or correctness problems in code changes - Get a structured summary of review findings
simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
session-navigation: Navigate, search, and manage Droid sessions. Use when the user wants to: - List recent sessions - Search session history for specific topics or patterns - Resume a previous session - Get details about what was accomplished in a session - Find sessions by project, date, or content
pdf-document: Produce polished PDF documents (reports, invoices, resumes, letters, flyers, certificates, any "export to PDF" deliverable). Use whenever the user asks for a PDF or a printable document.
powerpoint: Produce polished PowerPoint presentations (decks, slide shows, pitch decks, any "export to PowerPoint" deliverable). Use whenever the user asks for a PowerPoint, a slide deck, or a presentation.
excel: Produce polished Excel spreadsheets (reports, budgets, data exports, any "export to Excel" deliverable). Use whenever the user asks for an Excel file, a spreadsheet, or an .xlsx deliverable.
install-qa: Set up automated QA testing for this project. Performs deep codebase analysis, asks targeted questions, and generates a modular QA skill with sub-skills per app, a GitHub Actions workflow, and a report template. This is a complex, multi-phase process -- quality assurance is foundational and we take the time to get it right.
security-review: Security-focused code review using STRIDE, OWASP Top 10, OWASP LLM Top 10, and supply chain analysis. Use when: - Reviewing a PR for security vulnerabilities - Performing a security audit of code changes - Identifying injection, auth, data exposure, and other security issues - Running a full-project security audit reviewing every source file
deep-security-review: Correctness-first, depth-first security audit of a single repository. Invoked by /security-review when the user opts into "thorough" mode. Uses a heterogeneous multi-model jury (latest Opus + latest GPT + latest Gemini). A mandatory 3-pass floor (line-anchor verification, vendor prior-art, deep prior-art) runs against every Pass 0 lieutenant-seeded candidate; a conditional escalation tier (dataflow + reachability, exploit construction, adversarial red-team disprove) fires on per-finding triggers. Produces FINDINGS.md, JUDGE.md, STATUS.md, severity-sorted master list, and optional PoC + evidence artifacts for that repo. Never uploads or submits anything; all outputs local.
wiki: Generate comprehensive codebase documentation for a repository. Uploads the wiki to view in the Factory app.
wiki-video-gen: Generate Factory-branded HyperFrames video overviews for repository wikis. Use when wiki generation reaches Phase 3.6, or when the user asks for a narrated repository overview video.
install-wiki: Install a CI action that automatically refreshes the Factory Wiki on each push to the default branch. Use when the user wants to set up automated wiki generation, install wiki CI, or configure wiki refresh.
browse-wiki: Search and read wiki documentation for a repository
incident: RCA runbook for alerts. Given an alert link (or prompted to provide one), identifies the alert type, verifies tooling/auth, and walks through root cause analysis using deep research. Persists learnings to incident-guidelines for future reuse.
durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.

Repository files navigation # jax-js: JAX in pure JavaScript [Permalink: jax-js: JAX in pure JavaScript](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#jax-js-jax-in-pure-javascript) **[Website](https://jax-js.com/) \|** **[API Reference](https://jax-js.com/docs/) \|** **[Compatibility Table](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js/blob/main/FEATURES.md) \|** **[Discord](https://discord.gg/BW6YsCd4Tf)** **jax-js** is a machine learning framework for the browser. It aims to bring [JAX](https://jax.dev/)-style, high-performance CPU and GPU kernels to JavaScript, so you can run numerical applications on the web. ``` npm i @jax-js/jax ``` Under the hood, it translates array operations into a compiler representation, then synthesizes kernels in WebAssembly and WebGPU. The library is written from scratch, with zero external dependencies. It maintains close API compatibility with NumPy/JAX. Since everything runs client-side, jax-js is likely the most portable GPU ML framework, since it runs anywhere a browser can run. ## Quickstart [Permalink: Quickstart](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#quickstart) ``` import { numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax"; // Array operations, compatible with JAX/NumPy. const x = np.array([1, 2, 3]); const y = x.mul(4); // [4, 8, 12] ``` ### Web usage (CDN) [Permalink: Web usage (CDN)](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#web-usage-cdn) In vanilla JavaScript (without a bundler), just import from a module script tag. This is the easiest way to get started on a blank HTML page. ``` ``` ### Platforms [Permalink: Platforms](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#platforms) This table refers to latest versions of each browser. WebGPU has gained wide support in browsers as of late 2025. | Platform | CPU (Wasm) | GPU (WebGPU) | GPU (WebGL) | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Chrome / Edge | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Firefox | ✅ | ✅ \- macOS 26+ | ✅ | | Safari | ✅ | ✅ \- macOS 26+ | ✅ | | iOS | ✅ | ✅ \- iOS 26+ | ✅ | | Chrome for Android | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Firefox for Android | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | Node.js | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | Deno | ✅ | ✅ \- async | ❌ | ## Tutorial [Permalink: Tutorial](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#tutorial) Programming in `jax-js` looks [very similar to JAX](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/jax-101.html), just in JavaScript. ### Arrays [Permalink: Arrays](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#arrays) Create an array with `np.array()`: ``` import { numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax"; const ar = np.array([1, 2, 3]); ``` By default, this is a float32 array, but you can specify a different dtype: ``` const ar = np.array([1, 2, 3], { dtype: np.int32 }); ``` For more efficient construction, create an array from a JS `TypedArray` buffer: ``` const buf = new Float32Array([10, 20, 30, 100, 200, 300]); const ar = np.array(buf).reshape([2, 3]); ``` Once you're done with it, you can unwrap a `jax.Array` back into JavaScript. This will also apply any pending operations or lazy updates: ``` // 1) Returns a possibly nested JavaScript array. ar.js(); await ar.jsAsync(); // Faster, non-blocking // 2) Returns a flat TypedArray data buffer. ar.dataSync(); await ar.data(); // Fastest, non-blocking ``` Arrays can have mathematical operations applied to them. For example: ``` import { numpy as np, scipySpecial as special } from "@jax-js/jax"; const x = np.arange(100).astype(np.float32); // array of integers [0..99] const y1 = x.ref.add(x.ref); // x + x const y2 = np.sin(x.ref); // sin(x) const y3 = np.tanh(x.ref).mul(5); // 5 * tanh(x) const y4 = special.erfc(x.ref); // erfc(x) ``` Notice that in the above code, we used `x.ref`. This is because of the memory model, jax-js uses reference-counted _ownership_ to track when the memory of an Array can be freed. More on this below. ### Reference counting [Permalink: Reference counting](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#reference-counting) Big Arrays take up a lot of memory. Python ML libraries override the `__del__()` method to free memory, but JavaScript has no such API for running object destructors ( [cf.](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/FinalizationRegistry)). This means that you have to track references manually. jax-js tries to make this as ergonomic as possible, so you don't accidentally leak memory in a loop. Every `jax.Array` has a reference count. This satisfies the following rules: - Whenever you create an Array, its reference count starts at `1`. - When an Array's reference count reaches `0`, it is freed and can no longer be used. - Given an Array `a`: - Accessing `a.ref` returns `a` and changes its reference count by `+1`. - Passing `a` into any function as argument changes its reference count by `-1`. - Calling `a.dispose()` also changes its reference count by `-1`. What this means is that all functions in jax-js must _take ownership_ of their arguments as references. Whenever you would like to pass an Array as argument, you can pass it directly to dispose of it, or use `.ref` if you'd like to use it again later. **You must follow these rules on your own functions as well!** All combinators like `jvp`, `grad`, `jit` assume that you are following these conventions on how arguments are passed, and they will respect them as well. ``` // Bad: Uses `x` twice, decrementing its reference count twice. function foo_bad(x: np.Array, y: np.Array) { return x.add(x.mul(y)); } // Good: The first usage of `x` is `x.ref`, adding +1 to refcount. function foo_good(x: np.Array, y: np.Array) { return x.ref.add(x.mul(y)); } ``` Here's another example: ``` // Bad: Doesn't consume `x` in the `if`-branch. function bar_bad(x: np.Array, skip: boolean) { if (skip) return np.zeros(x.shape); return x; } // Good: Consumes `x` the one time in each branch. function bar_good(x: np.Array, skip: boolean) { if (skip) { const ret = np.zeros(x.shape); x.dispose(); return ret; } return x; } ``` You can assume that every function in jax-js takes ownership properly, except with a couple of very rare exceptions that are documented. ### grad(), vmap() and jit() [Permalink: grad(), vmap() and jit()](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#grad-vmap-and-jit) JAX's signature composable transformations are also supported in jax-js. Here is a simple example of using `grad` and `vmap` to compute the derivaive of a function: ``` import { numpy as np, grad, vmap } from "@jax-js/jax"; const x = np.linspace(-10, 10, 1000); const y1 = vmap(grad(np.sin))(x.ref); // d/dx sin(x) = cos(x) const y2 = np.cos(x); np.allclose(y1, y2); // => true ``` The `jit` function is especially useful when doing long sequences of primitives on GPU, since it fuses operations together into a single kernel dispatch. This [improves memory bandwidth usage](https://substack.com/home/post/p-163548742) on hardware accelerators, which is the bottleneck on GPU rather than raw FLOPs. For instance: ``` export const hypot = jit(function hypot(x1: np.Array, x2: np.Array) { return np.sqrt(np.square(x1).add(np.square(x2))); }); ``` Without JIT, the `hypot()` function would require four kernel dispatches: two multiplies, one add, and one sqrt. JIT fuses these together into a single kernel that does it all at once. All functional transformations can take typed `JsTree` of inputs and outputs. These are similar to [JAX's pytrees](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/pytrees.html), and it's basically just a structure of nested JavaScript objects and arrays. For instance: ``` import { grad, numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax"; type Params = { foo: np.Array; bar: np.Array[]; }; function getSums(p: Params) { const fooSum = p.foo.sum(); const barSum = p.bar.map((x) => x.sum()).reduce(np.add); return fooSum.add(barSum); } grad(getSums)({ foo: np.array([1, 2, 3]), bar: [np.array([10]), np.array([11, 12])], }); // => { foo: [1, 1, 1], bar: [[1], [1, 1]] } ``` Note that you need to use `type` alias syntax rather than `interface` to define fine-grained `JsTree` types. ### Devices [Permalink: Devices](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#devices) Similar to JAX, jax-js has a concept of "devices" which are a backend that stores Arrays in memory and determines how to execute compiled operations on them. There are currently 4 devices in jax-js: - `cpu`: Slow, interpreted JS, only meant for debugging. - `wasm`: [WebAssembly](https://webassembly.org/), multi-threaded when [`SharedArrayBuffer`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/SharedArrayBuffer) is available. - `webgpu`: [WebGPU](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGPU_API), available on [supported browsers](https://caniuse.com/webgpu) (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, iOS). - `webgl`: [WebGL2](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL2RenderingContext), via fragment shaders. This is an older graphics API that runs on almost all browsers, but it is much slower than WebGPU. It's offered on a best-effort basis and not as well-supported. **We recommend `webgpu` for best performance, especially when running neural networks.** The default device is `wasm`, but you can change this at startup time: ``` import { defaultDevice, init } from "@jax-js/jax"; const devices = await init(); // Starts all available backends. if (devices.includes("webgpu")) { defaultDevice("webgpu"); } else { console.warn("WebGPU is not supported, falling back to Wasm."); } ``` You can also place individual arrays on specific devices: ``` import { devicePut, numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax"; const ar = np.array([1, 2, 3]); // Starts with device="wasm" await devicePut(ar, "webgpu"); // Now device="webgpu" ``` ### Helper libraries [Permalink: Helper libraries](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#helper-libraries) There are other libraries in the `@jax-js` namespace that can work with jax-js, or be used in a self-contained way in other projects. - [**`@jax-js/loaders`**](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js/blob/main/packages/loaders) can load tensors from various formats like Safetensors, includes a fast and compliant implementation of BPE, and caches HTTP requests for large assets like model weights in OPFS. - [**`@jax-js/onnx`**](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js/blob/main/packages/onnx) is a model loader from the [ONNX](https://onnx.ai/) format into native jax-js functions. - [**`@jax-js/optax`**](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js/blob/main/packages/optax) provides implementations of optimizers like Adam and SGD. ### Performance [Permalink: Performance](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#performance) To see per-kernel traces in browser development tools, call `jax.profiler.startTrace()`. The WebGPU runtime includes an ML compiler with tile-aware optimizations, tuned for indiidual browsers. Also, this library uniquely has the `jit()` feature that fuses operations together and records an execution graph. jax-js achieves **over 7000 GFLOP/s** for matrix multiplication on an Apple M4 Max chip ( [try it](https://jax-js.com/bench/matmul)). For that example, it's significantly faster than both [TensorFlow.js](https://github.com/tensorflow/tfjs) and [ONNX Runtime Web](https://www.npmjs.com/package/onnxruntime-web), which both use handwritten libraries of custom kernels. It's still early though. There's a lot of low-hanging fruit to continue optimizing the library, as well as unique optimizations such as FlashAttention variants. ### API Reference [Permalink: API Reference](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#api-reference) That's all for this short tutorial. Please see the generated [API reference](https://jax-js.com/docs) for detailed documentation. ## Development [Permalink: Development](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#development) _The following technical details are for contributing to jax-js and modifying its internals._ This repository is managed by [`pnpm`](https://pnpm.io/). You can compile and build all packages in watch mode with: ``` pnpm install pnpm run build:watch ``` Then you can run tests in a headless browser using [Vitest](https://vitest.dev/). ``` pnpm exec playwright install pnpm test ``` We are currently on an older version of Playwright that supports using WebGPU in headless mode; newer versions skip the WebGPU tests. To start a Vite dev server running the website, demos and REPL: ``` pnpm -C website dev ``` You can run the linter, code formatter, and type checker with: ``` pnpm lint # Run ESLint pnpm format # Format all files with Prettier pnpm format:check # Check formatting without writing pnpm check # Run TypeScript type checking ``` ## Future work / help wanted [Permalink: Future work / help wanted](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#future-work--help-wanted) Contributions are welcomed! Some fruitful areas to look into: - Adding support for more JAX functions and operations, see [compatibility table](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js/blob/main/FEATURES.md). - Improving performance of the WebGPU and Wasm runtimes, generating better kernels, and using SIMD and multithreading. (Even single-threaded Wasm could be ~20x faster.) - Making a fast transformer inference engine, comparing against onnxruntime-web. You may join our [Discord server](https://discord.gg/BW6YsCd4Tf) and chat with the community. ## About JAX in JavaScript – ML library for the web, running on WebGPU & Wasm [jax-js.com](https://jax-js.com/ "https://jax-js.com") ### Topics [javascript](https://github.com/topics/javascript "Topic: javascript") [webgl](https://github.com/topics/webgl "Topic: webgl") [machine-learning](https://github.com/topics/machine-learning "Topic: machine-learning") [numpy](https://github.com/topics/numpy "Topic: numpy") [wasm](https://github.com/topics/wasm "Topic: wasm") [jit](https://github.com/topics/jit "Topic: jit") [neural-networks](https://github.com/topics/neural-networks "Topic: neural-networks") [webgpu](https://github.com/topics/webgpu "Topic: webgpu") [jax](https://github.com/topics/jax "Topic: jax") ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#readme-ov-file) ### License [MIT license](https://github.com/ekzhang/jax-js#MIT-1-ov-file) ### Uh oh! # jax-js: JAX in pure JavaScript

**[Website](https://jax-js.com/) \|**
**[API Reference](https://jax-js.com/docs/) \|**
**[Compatibility Table](https://www.jsdelivr.com/package/npm/@jax-js/FEATURES.md) \|**
**[Discord](https://discord.gg/BW6YsCd4Tf)**

**jax-js** is a machine learning framework for the browser. It aims to bring
[JAX](https://jax.dev/)-style, high-performance CPU and GPU kernels to JavaScript, so you can run
numerical applications on the web.

```bash
npm i @jax-js/jax
```

Under the hood, it translates array operations into a compiler representation, then synthesizes
kernels in WebAssembly and WebGPU.

The library is written from scratch, with zero external dependencies. It maintains close API
compatibility with NumPy/JAX. Since everything runs client-side, jax-js is likely the most portable
GPU ML framework, since it runs anywhere a browser can run.

## Quickstart

```js
import { numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax";

// Array operations, compatible with JAX/NumPy.
const x = np.array([1, 2, 3]);
const y = x.mul(4); // [4, 8, 12]
```

### Web usage (CDN)

In vanilla JavaScript (without a bundler), just import from a module script tag. This is the easiest
way to get started on a blank HTML page.

```html
<script type="module">
  import { numpy as np } from "https://esm.sh/@jax-js/jax";
</script>
```

### Platforms

This table refers to latest versions of each browser. WebGPU has gained wide support in browsers as
of late 2025.

\[object Object\]

|
|

## Examples

- [**g9-jaxjs**: Automatically interactive graphics with forward-mode AD](https://srush.github.io/g9jax/)
- [**autoresearch-webgpu**: autoresesarch, in the browser](https://autoresearch.lucasgelfond.online/)
- [**tanh.xyz**: Interactive ML visualizations](https://tanh.xyz/)
- [**jax-js-bayes**: Declarative Bayesian modeling library](https://github.com/StefanSko/jax-js-bayes)

Demos on the jax-js website:

- [Training neural networks on MNIST](https://jax-js.com/mnist)
- [Voice cloning: Kyutai Pocket TTS](https://jax-js.com/tts)
- [CLIP embeddings for books in-browser](https://jax-js.com/mobileclip)
- [Object detection: D-FINE (ONNX)](https://jax-js.com/d-fine)
- [Object detection: DETR ResNet-50 (ONNX)](https://jax-js.com/detr-resnet-50)
- [Fluid simulation (Navier-Stokes)](https://jax-js.com/fluid-sim)
- [In-browser REPL](https://jax-js.com/repl)
- [Matmul benchmark](https://jax-js.com/bench/matmul)
- [Matvec benchmark](https://jax-js.com/bench/matvec)
- [Conv2d benchmark](https://jax-js.com/bench/conv2d)
- [Mandelbrot set](https://jax-js.com/mandelbrot)

## Tutorial

Programming in `jax-js` looks [very similar to JAX](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/jax-101.html),
just in JavaScript.

### Arrays

Create an array with `np.array()`:

```ts
import { numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax";

const ar = np.array([1, 2, 3]);
```

By default, this is a float32 array, but you can specify a different dtype:

```ts
const ar = np.array([1, 2, 3], { dtype: np.int32 });
```

For more efficient construction, create an array from a JS `TypedArray` buffer:

```ts
const buf = new Float32Array([10, 20, 30, 100, 200, 300]);
const ar = np.array(buf).reshape([2, 3]);
```

Once you're done with it, you can unwrap a `jax.Array` back into JavaScript. This will also apply
any pending operations or lazy updates:

```ts
// 1) Returns a possibly nested JavaScript array.
ar.js();
await ar.jsAsync(); // Faster, non-blocking

// 2) Returns a flat TypedArray data buffer.
ar.dataSync();
await ar.data(); // Fastest, non-blocking
```

Arrays can have mathematical operations applied to them. For example:

```ts
import { numpy as np, scipySpecial as special } from "@jax-js/jax";

const x = np.arange(100).astype(np.float32); // array of integers [0..99]

const y1 = x.ref.add(x.ref); // x + x
const y2 = np.sin(x.ref); // sin(x)
const y3 = np.tanh(x.ref).mul(5); // 5 * tanh(x)
const y4 = special.erfc(x.ref); // erfc(x)
```

Notice that in the above code, we used `x.ref`. This is because of the memory model, jax-js uses
reference-counted _ownership_ to track when the memory of an Array can be freed. More on this below.

### Reference counting

Big Arrays take up a lot of memory. Python ML libraries override the `__del__()` method to free
memory, but JavaScript has no such API for running object destructors
( [cf.](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/FinalizationRegistry)).
This means that you have to track references manually. jax-js tries to make this as ergonomic as
possible, so you don't accidentally leak memory in a loop.

Every `jax.Array` has a reference count. This satisfies the following rules:

- Whenever you create an Array, its reference count starts at `1`.
- When an Array's reference count reaches `0`, it is freed and can no longer be used.
- Given an Array `a`:
  - Accessing `a.ref` returns `a` and changes its reference count by `+1`.
  - Passing `a` into any function as argument changes its reference count by `-1`.
  - Calling `a.dispose()` also changes its reference count by `-1`.

What this means is that all functions in jax-js must _take ownership_ of their arguments as
references. Whenever you would like to pass an Array as argument, you can pass it directly to
dispose of it, or use `.ref` if you'd like to use it again later.

**You must follow these rules on your own functions as well!** All combinators like `jvp`, `grad`,
`jit` assume that you are following these conventions on how arguments are passed, and they will
respect them as well.

```ts
// Bad: Uses `x` twice, decrementing its reference count twice.
function foo_bad(x: np.Array, y: np.Array) {
  return x.add(x.mul(y));
}

// Good: The first usage of `x` is `x.ref`, adding +1 to refcount.
function foo_good(x: np.Array, y: np.Array) {
  return x.ref.add(x.mul(y));
}
```

Here's another example:

```ts
// Bad: Doesn't consume `x` in the `if`-branch.
function bar_bad(x: np.Array, skip: boolean) {
  if (skip) return np.zeros(x.shape);
  return x;
}

// Good: Consumes `x` the one time in each branch.
function bar_good(x: np.Array, skip: boolean) {
  if (skip) {
    const ret = np.zeros(x.shape);
    x.dispose();
    return ret;
  }
  return x;
}
```

You can assume that every function in jax-js takes ownership properly, except with a couple of very
rare exceptions that are documented.

### grad(), vmap() and jit()

JAX's signature composable transformations are also supported in jax-js. Here is a simple example of
using `grad` and `vmap` to compute the derivaive of a function:

```ts
import { numpy as np, grad, vmap } from "@jax-js/jax";

const x = np.linspace(-10, 10, 1000);

const y1 = vmap(grad(np.sin))(x.ref); // d/dx sin(x) = cos(x)
const y2 = np.cos(x);

np.allclose(y1, y2); // => true
```

The `jit` function is especially useful when doing long sequences of primitives on GPU, since it
fuses operations together into a single kernel dispatch. This
[improves memory bandwidth usage](https://substack.com/home/post/p-163548742) on hardware
accelerators, which is the bottleneck on GPU rather than raw FLOPs. For instance:

```ts
export const hypot = jit(function hypot(x1: np.Array, x2: np.Array) {
  return np.sqrt(np.square(x1).add(np.square(x2)));
});
```

Without JIT, the `hypot()` function would require four kernel dispatches: two multiplies, one add,
and one sqrt. JIT fuses these together into a single kernel that does it all at once.

All functional transformations can take typed `JsTree` of inputs and outputs. These are similar to
[JAX's pytrees](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/pytrees.html), and it's basically just a structure of
nested JavaScript objects and arrays. For instance:

```ts
import { grad, numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax";

type Params = {
  foo: np.Array;
  bar: np.Array[];
};

function getSums(p: Params) {
  const fooSum = p.foo.sum();
  const barSum = p.bar.map((x) => x.sum()).reduce(np.add);
  return fooSum.add(barSum);
}

grad(getSums)({
  foo: np.array([1, 2, 3]),
  bar: [np.array([10]), np.array([11, 12])],
});
// => { foo: [1, 1, 1], bar: [[1], [1, 1]] }
```

Note that you need to use `type` alias syntax rather than `interface` to define fine-grained
`JsTree` types.

### Devices

Similar to JAX, jax-js has a concept of "devices" which are a backend that stores Arrays in memory
and determines how to execute compiled operations on them.

There are currently 4 devices in jax-js:

- `cpu`: Slow, interpreted JS, only meant for debugging.
- `wasm`: [WebAssembly](https://webassembly.org/), multi-threaded when
[`SharedArrayBuffer`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/SharedArrayBuffer)
is available.
- `webgpu`: [WebGPU](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGPU_API), available on
[supported browsers](https://caniuse.com/webgpu) (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, iOS).
- `webgl`: [WebGL2](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL2RenderingContext), via
fragment shaders. This is an older graphics API that runs on almost all browsers, but it is much
slower than WebGPU. It's offered on a best-effort basis and not as well-supported.

**We recommend `webgpu` for best performance, especially when running neural networks.** The default
device is `wasm`, but you can change this at startup time:

```ts
import { defaultDevice, init } from "@jax-js/jax";

const devices = await init(); // Starts all available backends.

if (devices.includes("webgpu")) {
  defaultDevice("webgpu");
} else {
  console.warn("WebGPU is not supported, falling back to Wasm.");
}
```

You can also place individual arrays on specific devices:

```ts
import { devicePut, numpy as np } from "@jax-js/jax";

const ar = np.array([1, 2, 3]); // Starts with device="wasm"
await devicePut(ar, "webgpu"); // Now device="webgpu"
```

### Helper libraries

There are other libraries in the `@jax-js` namespace that can work with jax-js, or be used in a
self-contained way in other projects.

- [**`@jax-js/loaders`**](https://www.jsdelivr.com/package/npm/@jax-js/packages/loaders) can load tensors from various formats like Safetensors,
includes a fast and compliant implementation of BPE, and caches HTTP requests for large assets
like model weights in OPFS.
- [**`@jax-js/onnx`**](https://www.jsdelivr.com/package/npm/@jax-js/packages/onnx) is a model loader from the [ONNX](https://onnx.ai/) format
into native jax-js functions.
- [**`@jax-js/optax`**](https://www.jsdelivr.com/package/npm/@jax-js/packages/optax) provides implementations of optimizers like Adam and SGD.

### Performance

To see per-kernel traces in browser development tools, call `jax.profiler.startTrace()`.

The WebGPU runtime includes an ML compiler with tile-aware optimizations, tuned for indiidual
browsers. Also, this library uniquely has the `jit()` feature that fuses operations together and
records an execution graph. jax-js achieves **over 7000 GFLOP/s** for matrix multiplication on an
Apple M4 Max chip ( [try it](https://jax-js.com/bench/matmul)).

For that example, it's significantly faster than both
[TensorFlow.js](https://github.com/tensorflow/tfjs) and
[ONNX Runtime Web](https://www.npmjs.com/package/onnxruntime-web), which both use handwritten
libraries of custom kernels.

It's still early though. There's a lot of low-hanging fruit to continue optimizing the library, as
well as unique optimizations such as FlashAttention variants.

### API Reference

That's all for this short tutorial. Please see the generated
[API reference](https://jax-js.com/docs) for detailed documentation.

## Development

_The following technical details are for contributing to jax-js and modifying its internals._

This repository is managed by [`pnpm`](https://pnpm.io/). You can compile and build all packages in
watch mode with:

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm run build:watch
```

Then you can run tests in a headless browser using [Vitest](https://vitest.dev/).

```bash
pnpm exec playwright install
pnpm test
```

We are currently on an older version of Playwright that supports using WebGPU in headless mode;
newer versions skip the WebGPU tests.

To start a Vite dev server running the website, demos and REPL:

```bash
pnpm -C website dev
```

You can run the linter, code formatter, and type checker with:

```bash
pnpm lint          # Run ESLint
pnpm format        # Format all files with Prettier
pnpm format:check  # Check formatting without writing
pnpm check         # Run TypeScript type checking
```

## Future work / help wanted

Contributions are welcomed! Some fruitful areas to look into:

- Adding support for more JAX functions and operations, see [compatibility table](https://www.jsdelivr.com/package/npm/@jax-js/FEATURES.md).
- Improving performance of the WebGPU and Wasm runtimes, generating better kernels, and using SIMD
and multithreading. (Even single-threaded Wasm could be ~20x faster.)
- Making a fast transformer inference engine, comparing against onnxruntime-web.

You may join our [Discord server](https://discord.gg/BW6YsCd4Tf) and chat with the community.

```
  const t = ...;
  const stepped = stepFn(pts, shape, t); // consumes pts
  const buf = await stepped.data(); // consumes stepped, Float32Array
  currentBuf = buf; // save for next frame
  render(buf, t);
  if (running) requestAnimationFrame(frame);
}
```

Hmm but this creates a new array each frame which has allocation overhead. For 3000 points it's fine though.

Actually, even simpler and more robust: do the whole thing in JS but use jax-js for the heavy vectorized projection math. OR, realize that for ~3000 points, plain JS is totally fine performance-wise, and the "using jax-js" requirement can be satisfied by doing the core field computation with jax-js np operations.

Let me reduce risk: I'll write the step computation using jax-js np operations, recreate the array each frame from the buffer (simple, correct, minor overhead acceptable for 3000 points). Use jit for the step function where possible.

Actually, recreating array each frame means jit can't capture the loop well, but each step is still jitted if I make stepFn = jit(...). But jit with a fresh array each time still works (it traces based on shape/dtype, not identity).

Hold on - let me reconsider. Maybe I'm overcomplicating. Let me check: does jax-js's `data()` consume? Let me look at the source or docs more. The README says: "ar.dataSync(); await ar.data(); // Fastest, non-blocking". And in ref counting section: "Passing an Array into any function as argument changes its reference count by -1. Calling a.dispose() also changes its reference count by -1."

It lists `data()` under "unwrap a jax.Array back into JavaScript". I believe `data()` consumes the array (it's like a function that takes the array as argument). To be safe, I'll use the recreate-from-buffer pattern. It's correct regardless.

Wait, actually there's an even simpler approach. Let me keep `pts` alive across frames using `.ref`:

```

async function frame() {
  const stepped = stepFn(pts.ref, shape, t); // pts.ref: refcount 2 -> passed to stepFn: refcount 1. stepped: refcount 1.
  const buf = await stepped.ref.data(); // stepped.ref: refcount 2 -> data() consumes one: refcount 1. buf = Float32Array
  render(buf, t);
  pts.dispose(); // old pts: refcount 1 -> 0, freed
  pts = stepped; // assign; stepped refcount stays 1 (we kept it via .ref before data)
  ...
}
```

Hmm wait, after `stepped.ref.data()`, stepped refcount went 1 -> 2 (ref) -> 1 (data consumes). Then `pts = stepped` just reassigns the variable, refcount still 1. Good. And old pts disposed. This keeps one persistent array. But it's error-prone.

You know what, the recreate-from-buffer pattern is safest. Let me go with that. The overhead is minimal. Let me also consider: maybe I don't even need to keep a jax array across frames. Each frame I create array from buffer, step it, get buffer back. Clean.

**Sphere projection:**

- target = normalize(x) * R
- np ops: `norms = np.sqrt(np.square(pts).sum(axis=1, keepdims=true))` then `target = pts / norms * R`

**Torus projection (approximate):**
- Torus in XY plane: major radius R, minor r.
- For a point, project to the ring then to the tube.
- d = sqrt(x^2 + y^2); u = (d - R); the nearest point on torus: 
  - angle = atan2(y, x); ring point = (R cos, R sin, 0); 
  - Then tube: direction from ring point to x in the plane spanned by radial and z.
  - Approximate: target = ringPoint + r * normalize(x - ringPoint)
- This needs elementwise ops. atan2, cos, sin available? jax-js has np.arctan2, np.cos, np.sin presumably.

- For a point (x,y,z), project onto the torus: find the nearest point on the torus
- The score is the direction toward the nearest point

   ```
   score = target_direction(points)  // gradient toward target
   points = points + step_size * score + noise * sigma(t)
   ```

The diffusion step function (not jitted initially, to avoid ref-counting complexity):
```js
function diffusionStep(points, target, t, key) {
  // t goes from 0 (noise) to 1 (clean)
  // sigma decreases from high to low
  const sigma = 0.3 * (1 - t);
  const stepSize = 0.05;
  
  // Score: direction toward target shape
  const direction = computeScore(points, target);
  
  // Update: move toward target + add decreasing noise
  const [noiseKey] = random.split(key);
  const noise = random.normal(noiseKey, points.shape).mul(sigma);
  
  const newPoints = points.add(direction.mul(stepSize)).add(noise);
  return newPoints;
}
```

Wait, I need to be careful. `points.add(...)` consumes `points`. If I want to keep using `points`, I need `points.ref`. But since I'm creating new points each step, I can let the old one be consumed.

1. **Sphere**: normalize each point to radius R

   - d = sqrt(u^2 + z^2)  (distance from torus surface)
   - direction = -normalize(p - nearest_point) * (d - r) / d
   
3. **Helix**: project onto a helix curve

Let me keep it to 2-3 shapes that cycle.

```tsx
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";
import { numpy as np, random, type Array as JaxArray } from "@jax-js/jax";

const N = 1500; // number of points
const SHAPES = ["sphere", "torus", "helix", "mobius"] as const;

// Generate shape point clouds as [N, 3] jax arrays
function spherePoints(n: number): JaxArray {
  // Fibonacci sphere
  const indices = np.arange(0, n).add(0.5);
  const phi = np.arccos(indices.mul(-1).div(n).add(1)); // arccos(1 - 2*(i+0.5)/n)
  const goldenAngle = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  const theta = indices.mul(goldenAngle);
  const r = 1.0;
  const x = np.sin(phi).mul(r);
  const y = np.cos(phi).mul(r);
  const z = np.sin(theta).mul(np.sin(phi)).mul(r);
  return np.stack([x, y, z]); // [N, 3]
}
```

```js
function sphereCoords(n: number): Float32Array {
  const coords = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const goldenAngle = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i + 0.5) / n;
    const phi = Math.acos(1 - 2 * t);
    const theta = i * goldenAngle;
    const r = 1.0;
    coords[i * 3] = Math.sin(phi) * Math.cos(theta) * r;
    coords[i * 3 + 1] = Math.cos(phi) * r;
    coords[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(phi) * Math.sin(theta) * r;
  }
  return coords;
}
```

Then: `const shape = np.array(coords).reshape([N, 3]);`

```js
function sphereCoords(n: number): Float32Array {
  const coords = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const goldenAngle = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i + 0.5) / n;
    const phi = Math.acos(1 - 2 * t);
    const theta = i * goldenAngle;
    const r = 1.0;
    coords[i * 3] = Math.sin(phi) * Math.cos(theta) * r;
    coords[i * 3 + 1] = Math.cos(phi) * r;
    coords[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(phi) * Math.sin(theta) * r;
  }
  return coords;
}
```

```tsx
"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

const N = 1000;
const TAU = Math.PI * 2;

// Diffusion schedule (cosine, from "Improved DDPM")

  const s = 0.008;
  return Math.cos(((t + s) / (1 + s)) * (Math.PI / 2)) ** 2;
};

// Target shapes: each returns Float32Array of shape [N*3]
type ShapeFn = (i: number, n: number) => [number, number, number];

// Torus knot
function torusKnot(n: number, R: number, r: number, p: number, q: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const x = (R + r * Math.cos(q * t)) * Math.cos(p * t);
    const y = (R + r * Math.cos(q * t)) * Math.sin(p * t);
    const z = r * Math.sin(q * t);
    pts[i * 3] = x;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = z;
  }
  return pts;
}

// Sphere (Fibonacci)
function fibSphere(n: number, r: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const phi = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const y = 1 - (i / (n - 1)) * 2;
    const r2 = Math.sqrt(1 - y * y);
    const theta = phi * i;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(theta) * r2 * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(theta) * r2 * r;
  }
  return pts;
}

  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);

    const ang = t * TAU * turns;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(ang) * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = (t - 0.5) * height;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(ang) * r;
  }
  return pts;
}
```

```tsx
"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

const N = 900;
const TAU = Math.PI * 2;

// Camera
const FOCAL = 900;
const CAM_Z = 700;
const CAM_TILT = -0.35;

// Diffusion cosine schedule
const alphaBar = (t: number) => {
  const s = 0.008;
  return Math.cos(((t + s) / (1 + s)) * (Math.PI / 2)) ** 2;
};

// Colors (slate-blue family matching the site)
const C = {
  node: "hsl(205, 25%, 60%)",
  glow: "hsl(200, 35%, 70%)",
  hot: "hsl(195, 40%, 72%)",
};

// Shape generators → Float32Array [N*3]
function torusKnot(n: number, R: number, r: number, p: number, q: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const cq = Math.cos(q * t);
    const x = (R + r * cq) * Math.cos(p * t);
    const y = r * Math.sin(q * t);
    const z = (R + r * cq) * Math.sin(p * t);
    pts[i * 3] = x;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = z;
  }
  return pts;
}

function fibSphere(n: number, r: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const phi = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const y = 1 - (i / (n - 1)) * 2;
    const r2 = Math.sqrt(1 - y * y);
    const theta = phi * i;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(theta) * r2 * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(theta) * r2 * r;
  }
  return pts;
}

function doubleHelix(n: number, r: number, height: number, turns: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = i / n;
    const ang = t * TAU * turns;
    const strand = i % 2 === 0 ? 0 : Math.PI;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(ang + strand) * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = (t - 0.5) * height;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(ang + strand) * r;
  }
  return pts;
}

function mobiusStrip(n: number, R: number, w: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const u = ((i % 20) / 20 - 0.5) * w; // spread across width
    const halfT = t / 2;
    pts[i * 3] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.cos(t);
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = u * Math.sin(halfT);
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.sin(t);
  }
  return pts;
}

// Shape configs — cycled through
const SHAPES = [
  () => torusKnot(N, 140, 50, 2, 3),
  () => fibSphere(N, 170),
  () => doubleHelix(N, 120, 320, 4),
  () => mobiusStrip(N, 140, 70),
];

export default function DiffusionField() {
  const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    const canvas = canvasRef.current;
    if (!canvas) return;
    const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d", { alpha: true });
    if (!ctx) return;

    let animationId = 0;
    let width = 0;
    let height = 0;
    let dpr = 1;
    let resizeTimer: ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null = null;
    let disposed = false;

    // jax-js state
    let jaxModule: typeof import("@jax-js/jax") | null = null;
    let noiseArr: Float32Array | null = null; // [N*3]
    let targetArr: Float32Array | null = null; // [N*3]
    let shapeIdx = 0;

    // Animation state
    let startTime = 0;
    const DENOISE_DUR = 6.0;  // t: 1→0 (generation)
    const HOLD_DUR = 1.5;     // hold at t=0
    const NOISE_DUR = 3.0;    // t: 0→1 (forward diffusion)
    const CYCLE_DUR = DENOISE_DUR + HOLD_DUR + NOISE_DUR;

    const resize = () => {
      dpr = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2);
      width = window.innerWidth;
      height = Math.min(380, Math.max(260, Math.floor(window.innerHeight * 0.38)));
      canvas.width = Math.floor(width * dpr);
      canvas.height = Math.floor(height * dpr);
      canvas.style.width = `${width}px`;
      canvas.style.height = `${height}px`;
      ctx.setTransform(dpr, 0, 0, dpr, 0, 0);
      ctx.fillStyle = "#000000";
      ctx.fillRect(0, 0, width, height);
    };

    const debouncedResize = () => {
      if (resizeTimer) clearTimeout(resizeTimer);
      resizeTimer = setTimeout(resize, 180);
    };

    // Generate new noise using jax-js
    async function genNoise(): Promise<Float32Array> {
      if (!jaxModule) return new Float32Array(N * 3);
      const { random } = jaxModule;
      const key = random.key(Math.floor(Math.random() * 2 ** 31));
      const noise = random.normal(key, [N, 3]);
      const data = await noise.data();
      noise.dispose();
      // Scale noise to match shape extent
      const f32 = new Float32Array(data as ArrayBuffer);
      // scale to ~180 spread
      const scale = 180;
      for (let i = 0; i < f32.length; i++) f32[i] *= scale;
      return f32;
    }

    async function setup() {
      try {
        jaxModule = await import("@jax-js/jax");
        const { init, defaultDevice } = jaxModule;
        const devices = await init();
        if (devices.includes("webgpu")) {
          defaultDevice("webgpu");
        } else if (devices.includes("wasm")) {
          defaultDevice("wasm");
        }
      } catch (e) {
        console.warn("jax-js init failed, using fallback", e);
      }

      resize();
      targetArr = SHAPES[shapeIdx]();
      noiseArr = await genNoise();
      startTime = performance.now();
      animationId = requestAnimationFrame(draw);
    }

    const project = (wx: number, wy: number, wz: number, cosY: number, sinY: number, cosT: number, sinT: number) => {
      const rx = wx * cosY - wz * sinY;
      const rz = wx * sinY + wz * cosY;
      const ty = wy * cosT - rz * sinT;
      const tz = wy * sinT + rz * cosT;
      const denom = CAM_Z + tz;
      const safeDenom = denom > 1 ? denom : 1;
      const scale = FOCAL / safeDenom;
      return { sx: width / 2 + rx * scale, sy: height / 2 + ty * scale, depth: tz, scale };
    };

    const draw = async (now: number) => {
      if (disposed) return;
      const elapsed = (now - startTime) / 1000;
      const cycleTime = elapsed % CYCLE_DUR;
      const cycleNum = Math.floor(elapsed / CYCLE_DUR);

      // Check if we need to switch shape (at the start of a new cycle)
      // ... handle shape switching

      // Compute diffusion time t
      let t: number;
      if (cycleTime < DENOISE_DUR) {
        t = 1 - cycleTime / DENOISE_DUR; // 1→0
      } else if (cycleTime < DENOISE_DUR + HOLD_DUR) {
        t = 0;
      } else {
        t = (cycleTime - DENOISE_DUR - HOLD_DUR) / NOISE_DUR; // 0→1
      }

      const ab = alphaBar(t);
      const sqrtA = Math.sqrt(ab);
      const sqrtN = Math.sqrt(1 - ab);

      // Compute interpolated positions
      // x = sqrtA * target + sqrtN * noise
      const positions = new Float32Array(N * 3);
      if (targetArr && noiseArr) {
        for (let i = 0; i < N * 3; i++) {
          positions[i] = sqrtA * targetArr[i] + sqrtN * noiseArr[i];
        }
      }

      // ... but wait, I wanted to use jax-js for this!
    };
  });
}
```

"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

const N = 900;
const TAU = Math.PI * 2;
const FOCAL = 900;
const CAM_Z = 700;
const CAM_TILT = -0.38;
const NOISE_SCALE = 120;

// Cosine diffusion schedule (Improved DDPM)
function alphaBar(t: number): number {
  const s = 0.008;
  return Math.cos(((t + s) / (1 + s)) * (Math.PI / 2)) ** 2;
}

const easeInOut = (x: number) =>
  x < 0.5 ? 2 * x * x : 1 - Math.pow(-2 * x + 2, 2) / 2;
const easeOut = (x: number) => 1 - Math.pow(1 - x, 3);

// --- Shape generators ---

interface ShapeData {
  points: Float32Array; // [N*3]
  connections: Int32Array; // [K*2] index pairs
}

function torusKnotShape(n: number, R: number, r: number, p: number, q: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const connections = new Int32Array(n * 2);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const cq = Math.cos(q * t);
    points[i * 3] = (R + r * cq) * Math.cos(p * t);
    points[i * 3 + 1] = r * Math.sin(q * t);
    points[i * 3 + 2] = (R + r * cq) * Math.sin(p * t);
    connections[i * 2] = i;
    connections[i * 2 + 1] = (i + 1) % n;
  }
  return { points, connections };
}

function sphereShape(n: number, r: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const phi = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const y = 1 - (i / (n - 1)) * 2;
    const r2 = Math.sqrt(1 - y * y);
    const theta = phi * i;
    points[i * 3] = Math.cos(theta) * r2 * r;
    points[i * 3 + 1] = y * r;
    points[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(theta) * r2 * r;
  }
  // Find 3 nearest neighbors for each point
  const connections = nearestNeighbors(points, n, 3);
  return { points, connections };
}

function helixShape(n: number, r: number, height: number, turns: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const connections = new Int32Array(n * 2);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = i / n;
    const ang = t * TAU * turns;
    const strand = i % 2 === 0 ? 0 : Math.PI;
    points[i * 3] = Math.cos(ang + strand) * r;
    points[i * 3 + 1] = (t - 0.5) * height;
    points[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(ang + strand) * r;
    connections[i * 2] = i;
    connections[i * 2 + 1] = (i + 2) % n; // connect within same strand
  }
  return { points, connections };
}

function mobiusShape(n: number, R: number, w: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const connections = new Int32Array(n * 2);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const u = ((i % 15) / 15 - 0.5) * w;
    const halfT = t / 2;
    points[i * 3] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.cos(t);
    points[i * 3 + 1] = u * Math.sin(halfT) * 2;
    points[i * 3 + 2] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.sin(t);
    connections[i * 2] = i;
    connections[i * 2 + 1] = (i + 1) % n;
  }
  return { points, connections };
}

function nearestNeighbors(points: Float32Array, n: number, k: number): Int32Array {
  // Simple O(n²) k-NN for small n
  const pairs: number[] = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const dists: Array<{ d: number; j: number }> = [];
    for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      if (i === j) continue;
      const dx = points[i*3] - points[j*3];
      const dy = points[i*3+1] - points[j*3+1];
      const dz = points[i*3+2] - points[j*3+2];
      dists.push({ d: dx*dx + dy*dy + dz*dz, j });
    }
    dists.sort((a, b) => a.d - b.d);
    for (let kk = 0; kk < k; kk++) {
      pairs.push(i, dists[kk].j);
    }
  }
  return Int32Array.from(pairs);
}

];

  bright: "hsl(195, 38%, 70%)",
  edge: "hsl(205, 20%, 45%)",
};

```tsx
"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

const N = 1000;
const TAU = Math.PI * 2;

// Diffusion schedule (cosine, from "Improved DDPM")

  const s = 0.008;
  return Math.cos(((t + s) / (1 + s)) * (Math.PI / 2)) ** 2;

// Torus knot
function torusKnot(n: number, R: number, r: number, p: number, q: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const x = (R + r * Math.cos(q * t)) * Math.cos(p * t);
    const y = (R + r * Math.cos(q * t)) * Math.sin(p * t);
    const z = r * Math.sin(q * t);
    pts[i * 3] = x;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = z;
  }
  return pts;
}

// Sphere (Fibonacci)
function fibSphere(n: number, r: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const phi = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const y = 1 - (i / (n - 1)) * 2;
    const r2 = Math.sqrt(1 - y * y);
    const theta = phi * i;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(theta) * r2 * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(theta) * r2 * r;
  }
  return pts;
}

// Helix

  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = i / n;
    const ang = t * TAU * turns;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(ang) * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = (t - 0.5) * height;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(ang) * r;
  }
  return pts;
}
```

```tsx
"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

const N = 900;
const TAU = Math.PI * 2;

// Camera
const FOCAL = 900;
const CAM_Z = 700;
const CAM_TILT = -0.35;

// Diffusion cosine schedule
const alphaBar = (t: number) => {
  const s = 0.008;
  return Math.cos(((t + s) / (1 + s)) * (Math.PI / 2)) ** 2;
};

// Colors (slate-blue family matching the site)
const C = {
  node: "hsl(205, 25%, 60%)",
  glow: "hsl(200, 35%, 70%)",
  hot: "hsl(195, 40%, 72%)",
};

// Shape generators → Float32Array [N*3]
function torusKnot(n: number, R: number, r: number, p: number, q: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const cq = Math.cos(q * t);
    const x = (R + r * cq) * Math.cos(p * t);
    const y = r * Math.sin(q * t);
    const z = (R + r * cq) * Math.sin(p * t);
    pts[i * 3] = x;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = z;
  }
  return pts;
}

function fibSphere(n: number, r: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const phi = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const y = 1 - (i / (n - 1)) * 2;
    const r2 = Math.sqrt(1 - y * y);
    const theta = phi * i;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(theta) * r2 * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = y * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(theta) * r2 * r;
  }
  return pts;
}

function doubleHelix(n: number, r: number, height: number, turns: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = i / n;
    const ang = t * TAU * turns;
    const strand = i % 2 === 0 ? 0 : Math.PI;
    pts[i * 3] = Math.cos(ang + strand) * r;
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = (t - 0.5) * height;
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(ang + strand) * r;
  }
  return pts;
}

function mobiusStrip(n: number, R: number, w: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const u = ((i % 20) / 20 - 0.5) * w; // spread across width
    const halfT = t / 2;
    pts[i * 3] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.cos(t);
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = u * Math.sin(halfT);
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.sin(t);
  }
  return pts;
}

// Shape configs — cycled through
const SHAPES = [
  () => torusKnot(N, 140, 50, 2, 3),
  () => fibSphere(N, 170),
  () => doubleHelix(N, 120, 320, 4),
  () => mobiusStrip(N, 140, 70),
];

export default function DiffusionField() {
  const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    const canvas = canvasRef.current;
    if (!canvas) return;
    const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d", { alpha: true });
    if (!ctx) return;

    let animationId = 0;
    let width = 0;
    let height = 0;
    let dpr = 1;
    let resizeTimer: ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null = null;
    let disposed = false;

    // jax-js state
    let jaxModule: typeof import("@jax-js/jax") | null = null;
    let noiseArr: Float32Array | null = null; // [N*3]
    let targetArr: Float32Array | null = null; // [N*3]
    let shapeIdx = 0;

    // Animation state
    let startTime = 0;
    const DENOISE_DUR = 6.0;  // t: 1→0 (generation)
    const HOLD_DUR = 1.5;     // hold at t=0
    const NOISE_DUR = 3.0;    // t: 0→1 (forward diffusion)
    const CYCLE_DUR = DENOISE_DUR + HOLD_DUR + NOISE_DUR;

    const resize = () => {
      dpr = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2);
      width = window.innerWidth;
      height = Math.min(380, Math.max(260, Math.floor(window.innerHeight * 0.38)));
      canvas.width = Math.floor(width * dpr);
      canvas.height = Math.floor(height * dpr);
      canvas.style.width = `${width}px`;
      canvas.style.height = `${height}px`;
      ctx.setTransform(dpr, 0, 0, dpr, 0, 0);
      ctx.fillStyle = "#000000";
      ctx.fillRect(0, 0, width, height);
    };

    const debouncedResize = () => {
      if (resizeTimer) clearTimeout(resizeTimer);
      resizeTimer = setTimeout(resize, 180);
    };

    // Generate new noise using jax-js
    async function genNoise(): Promise<Float32Array> {
      if (!jaxModule) return new Float32Array(N * 3);
      const { random } = jaxModule;

      return f32;
    }

      try {
        jaxModule = await import("@jax-js/jax");
        const { init, defaultDevice } = jaxModule;
        const devices = await init();
        if (devices.includes("webgpu")) {
          defaultDevice("webgpu");
        } else if (devices.includes("wasm")) {
          defaultDevice("wasm");
        }
      } catch (e) {
        console.warn("jax-js init failed, using fallback", e);

      targetArr = SHAPES[shapeIdx]();
      noiseArr = await genNoise();
      startTime = performance.now();
      animationId = requestAnimationFrame(draw);

      const rx = wx * cosY - wz * sinY;
      const rz = wx * sinY + wz * cosY;
      const ty = wy * cosT - rz * sinT;
      const tz = wy * sinT + rz * cosT;
      const denom = CAM_Z + tz;
      const safeDenom = denom > 1 ? denom : 1;
      const scale = FOCAL / safeDenom;
      return { sx: width / 2 + rx * scale, sy: height / 2 + ty * scale, depth: tz, scale };
    };

    const draw = async (now: number) => {
      if (disposed) return;
      const elapsed = (now - startTime) / 1000;
      const cycleTime = elapsed % CYCLE_DUR;
      const cycleNum = Math.floor(elapsed / CYCLE_DUR);

      // Check if we need to switch shape (at the start of a new cycle)
      // ... handle shape switching

      // Compute diffusion time t

      if (cycleTime < DENOISE_DUR) {
        t = 1 - cycleTime / DENOISE_DUR; // 1→0
      } else if (cycleTime < DENOISE_DUR + HOLD_DUR) {
        t = 0;
      } else {
        t = (cycleTime - DENOISE_DUR - HOLD_DUR) / NOISE_DUR; // 0→1
      }

      const ab = alphaBar(t);
      const sqrtA = Math.sqrt(ab);
      const sqrtN = Math.sqrt(1 - ab);

      // Compute interpolated positions
      // x = sqrtA * target + sqrtN * noise
      const positions = new Float32Array(N * 3);
      if (targetArr && noiseArr) {
        for (let i = 0; i < N * 3; i++) {
          positions[i] = sqrtA * targetArr[i] + sqrtN * noiseArr[i];
        }
      }

      // ... but wait, I wanted to use jax-js for this!
    };
  });
}
```

```tsx
"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

const N = 900;
const TAU = Math.PI * 2;
const FOCAL = 900;
```

  return Math.cos(((t + s) / (1 + s)) * (Math.PI / 2)) ** 2;
}

const easeInOut = (x: number) =>

const easeOut = (x: number) => 1 - Math.pow(1 - x, 3);

// --- Shape generators ---

interface ShapeData {
  points: Float32Array; // [N*3]
  connections: Int32Array; // [K*2] index pairs
}

function torusKnotShape(n: number, R: number, r: number, p: number, q: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const connections = new Int32Array(n * 2);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const cq = Math.cos(q * t);
    points[i * 3] = (R + r * cq) * Math.cos(p * t);
    points[i * 3 + 1] = r * Math.sin(q * t);
    points[i * 3 + 2] = (R + r * cq) * Math.sin(p * t);
    connections[i * 2] = i;
    connections[i * 2 + 1] = (i + 1) % n;
  }
  return { points, connections };
}

function sphereShape(n: number, r: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const phi = Math.PI * (3 - Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const y = 1 - (i / (n - 1)) * 2;
    const r2 = Math.sqrt(1 - y * y);
    const theta = phi * i;
    points[i * 3] = Math.cos(theta) * r2 * r;
    points[i * 3 + 1] = y * r;
    points[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(theta) * r2 * r;
  }
  // Find 3 nearest neighbors for each point
  const connections = nearestNeighbors(points, n, 3);
  return { points, connections };
}

function helixShape(n: number, r: number, height: number, turns: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const connections = new Int32Array(n * 2);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = i / n;
    const ang = t * TAU * turns;
    const strand = i % 2 === 0 ? 0 : Math.PI;
    points[i * 3] = Math.cos(ang + strand) * r;
    points[i * 3 + 1] = (t - 0.5) * height;
    points[i * 3 + 2] = Math.sin(ang + strand) * r;
    connections[i * 2] = i;
    connections[i * 2 + 1] = (i + 2) % n; // connect within same strand
  }
  return { points, connections };
}

function mobiusShape(n: number, R: number, w: number): ShapeData {
  const points = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const connections = new Int32Array(n * 2);
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const t = (i / n) * TAU;
    const u = ((i % 15) / 15 - 0.5) * w;
    const halfT = t / 2;
    points[i * 3] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.cos(t);
    points[i * 3 + 1] = u * Math.sin(halfT) * 2;
    points[i * 3 + 2] = (R + u * Math.cos(halfT)) * Math.sin(t);
    connections[i * 2] = i;
    connections[i * 2 + 1] = (i + 1) % n;
  }
  return { points, connections };
}

function nearestNeighbors(points: Float32Array, n: number, k: number): Int32Array {
  // Simple O(n²) k-NN for small n
  const pairs: number[] = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const dists: Array<{ d: number; j: number }> = [];
    for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      if (i === j) continue;
      const dx = points[i*3] - points[j*3];
      const dy = points[i*3+1] - points[j*3+1];
      const dz = points[i*3+2] - points[j*3+2];
      dists.push({ d: dx*dx + dy*dy + dz*dz, j });
    }
    dists.sort((a, b) => a.d - b.d);
    for (let kk = 0; kk < k; kk++) {
      pairs.push(i, dists[kk].j);
    }
  }
  return Int32Array.from(pairs);
}

I need to fix several issues:

- A number `n` means to access the `n`-th element along that axis, removing that axis from the resulting shape.
- An empty array `[]` means to keep that axis as-is, like `:` in Python.
- `null` means to add a new axis at that position, like `np.newaxis`.

So for `x[:,0]` I need to keep axis 0 (`[]`) and take element 0 on axis 1 (`0`). That would be `x.slice([], 0)`.

My current `x.slice(0, [], null)` is wrong - that would take element 0 on axis 0 (collapsing it), keep axis 1, and add a new axis. That gives shape [3,1] not [N].

1. The slice syntax. According to the docs:
- A number `n` means to access the `n`-th element along that axis, removing that axis from the resulting shape.
- An empty array `[]` means to keep that axis as-is, like `:` in Python.
- `null` means to add a new axis at that position, like `np.newaxis`.

So for `x[:,0]` I need to keep axis 0 (`[]`) and take element 0 on axis 1 (`0`). That would be `x.slice([], 0)`.

My current `x.slice(0, [], null)` is wrong - that would take element 0 on axis 0 (collapsing it), keep axis 1, and add a new axis. That gives shape [3,1] not [N].

content.text:
点群アニメーション(Transformer風ParticleField)を削除し、jax-jsで駆動する3D拡散アニメーションに置き換えました。

## 変更内容

**新規作成: `app/components/DiffusionField.tsx`**
- 1,200個の粒子がjax-js上でベクトル化されたLangevin拡散(SDE)を計算します
- 各ステップでオーバーダンプトンジャンジナミクスを実行: `dx = (-κ·x + α·cross(x,axis))·dt + σ·√dt·N(0,1)`
- 調和拘束(OUドリフト) + 渦巻きアドベクション場でカオス的な3D軌道を生成
- 全配列演算を`jit()`で1つのWebGPU/Wasmカーネルにフュージョン(高速化)
- WebGPU非対応時はWasmに自動フォールバック、jax-js初期化失敗時は静的グラデーション
- 位置をJSに読み戻し、Y軸回転+X軸チルトの透視投影で2Dキャンバスに描画
- フレーム間ストリーク線+半透明オーバーレイでリッチなモーショントレイル
- `import("@jax-js/jax")`を`useEffect`内で動的実行し、静的エクスポート(SSG)を維持

**更新: `app/page.tsx`**
- `ParticleField`のインポートと使用を`DiffusionField`に差し替え

**依存関係:** `@jax-js/jax@0.1.13`を追加

## 検証結果
- TypeScript型チェック: 合格
- ESLint: 合格
- `next build`(静的エクスポート): 合格
- ブラウザ(Playwright)でレンダリング確認: 粒子がスレートブルー系カラーで描画、コンソールエラーなし

`ParticleField.tsx`は現在未使用ですが、ユーザー所有物のため意図的に残しています(必要なら削除指示してください)。

content.thinking:
The user wants me to change the animation so that:
1. The image (particles) get compressed into a normal distribution
2. Then diffused and return back

This is essentially describing a diffusion process that goes both ways - first forward (compressing into noise/normal distribution) then reverse (diffusing back to original state). This is a classic forward/reverse diffusion process like in diffusion models.

Let me think about this:
- Forward process: particles gradually get perturbed by noise until they become a standard normal distribution N(0,1)
- Reverse process: particles gradually denoise back to their original structured positions

So I need to:
1. Have an "original" target image/structure (some shape or pattern)
2. Forward diffusion: gradually add noise to transform it into N(0,I)
3. Reverse diffusion: gradually remove noise to return to the original structure
4. Loop this process

This is a beautiful visualization of the diffusion process. Let me think about the implementation:

The key insight is that in a diffusion model:
- Forward: x_t = sqrt(1-beta_t) * x_{t-1} + sqrt(beta_t) * noise, gradually turning structured data into pure noise
- Reverse: gradually denoise from pure noise back to structured data

For a visualization, I can:
1. Define a target shape (e.g., a 3D shape like a torus, sphere, or some interesting pattern, maybe even the letters or a logo)
2. Phase 1 (Forward diffusion): Start from the target shape, progressively add noise until it becomes N(0,1)
3. Phase 2 (Reverse diffusion): From the noise, progressively interpolate back to the target shape

For the reverse process in a visualization (not actual ML training), I can use a simple interpolation:
- Forward: x_t = sqrt(1-beta_cumulative) * target + sqrt(1-(1-beta_cumulative)) * noise
  Actually, the closed form is: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t) * noise
  where alpha_bar_t = product of (1-beta_i) from i=1 to t
  
- Reverse: We can just reverse the interpolation, going from noise back to target

```typescript
"use client";

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";

// 3D diffusion field powered by jax-js.
// 
// Visualizes the forward and reverse diffusion process:
// 1. A structured 3D shape (sphere) gradually dissolves into N(0,1) noise
// 2. The noise gradually crystallizes back into the structured shape
// 3. Loops continuously
//
// The diffusion follows: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar) * x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar) * noise
// with a cosine schedule for alpha_bar.
// Noise generation and interpolation use jax-js (WebGPU/Wasm).

const TAU = Math.PI * 2;
const N_PARTICLES = 1500;
const SPHERE_R = 2.0;

// Phase durations (seconds)
const T_FORWARD = 2.5;
const T_HOLD_NOISE = 0.6;
const T_REVERSE = 2.5;
const T_HOLD_SHAPE = 0.8;
const T_CYCLE = T_FORWARD + T_HOLD_NOISE + T_REVERSE + T_HOLD_SHAPE;

// Colors
const COL_CORE = "hsl(205, 32%, 66%)";
const COL_HALO = "hsl(205, 26%, 52%)";
const COL_HOT = "hsl(198, 42%, 76%)";
const COL_NOISE = "hsl(220, 20%, 48%)"; // slightly different hue for noise phase

type JaxArray = import("@jax-js/jax").Array;
type JaxModule = typeof import("@jax-js/jax");

// Generate sphere positions using golden spiral sampling
function generateSphere(n: number, r: number): Float32Array {
  const pts = new Float32Array(n * 3);
  const goldenAngle = Math.PI * (1 + Math.sqrt(5));
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const phi = Math.acos(1 - 2 * (i + 0.5) / n);
    const theta = goldenAngle * (i + 0.5);
    pts[i * 3] = r * Math.sin(phi) * Math.cos(theta);
    pts[i * 3 + 1] = r * Math.sin(phi) * Math.sin(theta);
    pts[i * 3 + 2] = r * Math.cos(phi);
  }
  return pts;
}

// Cosine schedule: s=0 → alpha_bar=1, s=1 → alpha_bar=0
function alphaBar(s: number): number {
  const clamped = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, s));
  return Math.cos(clamped * Math.PI / 2) ** 2;
}

export default function DiffusionField() {
  const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    const canvas = canvasRef.current;
    if (!canvas) return;
    const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d", { alpha: true });
    if (!ctx) return;

    let animationId = 0;
    let disposed = false;
    let width = 0;
    let height = 0;
    let dpr = 1;
    let resizeTimer: ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null = null;

    let jax: JaxModule | null = null;
    let key: JaxArray | null = null;
    let targetArr: JaxArray | null = null;
    let noiseArr: JaxArray | null = null;
    let interpJit: ((target: JaxArray, noise: JaxArray, s: number, n: number) => JaxArray) | null = null;
    let prevScreen: Float32Array | null = null;
    let cycleStart = 0;

    const resize = () => {
      dpr = Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2);
      width = window.innerWidth;
      height = Math.min(420, Math.max(280, Math.floor(window.innerHeight * 0.42)));
      canvas.width = Math.floor(width * dpr);
      canvas.height = Math.floor(height * dpr);
      canvas.style.width = `${width}px`;
      canvas.style.height = `${height}px`;
      ctx.setTransform(dpr, 0, 0, dpr, 0, 0);
      ctx.fillStyle = "#000000";
      ctx.fillRect(0, 0, width, height);
      prevScreen = null;
    };

    const debouncedResize = () => {
      if (resizeTimer) clearTimeout(resizeTimer);
      resizeTimer = setTimeout(resize, 180);
    };

    const setupJax = async () => {
      jax = await import("@jax-js/jax");
      const np = jax.numpy;
      const random = jax.random;
      const { jit } = jax;

      try {
        const devices = await jax.init();
        if (devices.includes("webgpu")) jax.defaultDevice("webgpu");
      } catch {
        // fall back to wasm
      }

      // PRNG key
      key = random.key(20260618);

      // Target shape: sphere via golden spiral
      const sphereData = generateSphere(N_PARTICLES, SPHERE_R);
      targetArr = np.array(sphereData, { dtype: np.float32 }).reshape([N_PARTICLES, 3]);

      // Generate initial noise
      const [k0, k1] = random.split(key, 2);
      key = k0;
      noiseArr = random.normal(k1, [N_PARTICLES, 3]);

      // JIT-compiled interpolation: x = s * target + n * noise
      interpJit = jit(function interp(target: JaxArray, noise: JaxArray, s: number, n: number): JaxArray {
        return target.mul(s).add(noise.mul(n));
      });

      cycleStart = performance.now();
    };

    const regenerateNoise = () => {
      if (!jax || !key) return;
      const [k0, k1] = jax.random.split(key, 2);
      key = k0;
      try { noiseArr?.dispose(); } catch { /* ignore */ }
      noiseArr = jax.random.normal(k1, [N_PARTICLES, 3]);
    };

    const project = (wx, wy, wz, cosY, sinY, cosT, sinT, cx, cy) => {
      const rx = wx * cosY - wz * sinY;
      const rz = wx * sinY + wz * cosY;
      const ty = wy * cosT - rz * sinT;
      const tz = wy * sinT + rz * cosT;
      const denom = 9.5 + tz;
      const scale = 760 / (denom > 0.1 ? denom : 0.1);
      return { sx: cx + rx * scale, sy: cy + ty * scale, depth: tz, scale };
    };

    const draw = async () => {
      if (!jax || !interpJit || !targetArr || !noiseArr || !key) {
        if (!disposed) animationId = requestAnimationFrame(draw);
        return;
      }

      const now = performance.now();
      let elapsed = (now - cycleStart) * 0.001;
      
      // Loop the cycle
      if (elapsed > T_CYCLE) {
        cycleStart = now;
        elapsed = 0;
        regenerateNoise();
      }

      // Determine phase and s value
      let s: number; // 0 = structured, 1 = noise
      let phase: "forward" | "hold_noise" | "reverse" | "hold_shape";
      
      if (elapsed < T_FORWARD) {
        phase = "forward";
        s = elapsed / T_FORWARD;
      } else if (elapsed < T_FORWARD + T_HOLD_NOISE) {
        phase = "hold_noise";
        s = 1;
      } else if (elapsed < T_FORWARD + T_HOLD_NOISE + T_REVERSE) {
        phase = "reverse";
        s = 1 - (elapsed - T_FORWARD - T_HOLD_NOISE) / T_REVERSE;
      } else {
        phase = "hold_shape";
        s = 0;
      }

      // Apply easing for smoother transitions
      // Ease in-out for forward and reverse
      const easedS = easeInOutCubic(s);

      const ab = alphaBar(easedS);
      const signalScale = Math.sqrt(ab);
      const noiseScale = Math.sqrt(1 - ab);

      // Compute interpolated positions using jax-js
      let pos: Float32Array;
      try {
        const x = interpJit(targetArr.ref, noiseArr.ref, signalScale, noiseScale);
        pos = (await x.data()) as Float32Array;
        x.dispose();
      } catch {
        if (!disposed) animationId = requestAnimationFrame(draw);
        return;
      }

      // Fade prior frame
      ctx.globalAlpha = 1;
      ctx.fillStyle = "rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3)";
      ctx.fillRect(0, 0, width, height);

      const cx = width / 2;
      const cy = height / 2;
      const t = now * 0.001;
      const rotY = t * 0.12;
      const cosY = Math.cos(rotY);
      const sinY = Math.sin(rotY);
      const tilt = -0.34;
      const cosT = Math.cos(tilt);
      const sinT = Math.sin(tilt);

      // Color modulation based on phase
      const structureLevel = 1 - easedS; // 1 = structured, 0 = noise
      const coreCol = structureLevel > 0.5 ? COL_CORE : COL_NOISE;
      const hotCol = COL_HOT;

      type Item = { sx: number; sy: number; r: number; a: number; depth: number; psx: number; psy: number };
      const items: Item[] = [];
      const prev = prevScreen;

      for (let i = 0; i < N_PARTICLES; i++) {
        const p = project(pos[i*3], pos[i*3+1], pos[i*3+2], cosY, sinY, cosT, sinT, cx, cy);
        const depthFade = 1 - Math.min(1, Math.max(0, (p.depth - 2) / 9)) * 0.6;
        const r = Math.max(0.4, 1.0 + p.scale * 0.045);
        items.push({
          sx: p.sx, sy: p.sy, r, a: depthFade, depth: p.depth,
          psx: prev ? prev[i*2] : p.sx,
          psy: prev ? prev[i*2+1] : p.sy,
        });
      }

      items.sort((a, b) => b.depth - a.depth);

      for (const it of items) {
        if (prev && (Math.abs(it.sx - it.psx) > 0.3 || Math.abs(it.sy - it.psy) > 0.3)) {
          ctx.globalAlpha = it.a * 0.3;
          ctx.strokeStyle = COL_HALO;
          ctx.lineWidth = it.r * 0.85;
          ctx.beginPath();
          ctx.moveTo(it.psx, it.psy);
          ctx.lineTo(it.sx, it.sy);
          ctx.stroke();
        }
        ctx.globalAlpha = it.a * 0.18;
        ctx.fillStyle = COL_HALO;
        ctx.beginPath();
        ctx.arc(it.sx, it.sy, it.r * 2.6, 0, TAU);
        ctx.fill();
        ctx.globalAlpha = it.a * 0.85 * (0.5 + structureLevel * 0.5);
        ctx.fillStyle = coreCol;
        ctx.beginPath();
        ctx.arc(it.sx, it.sy, it.r, 0, TAU);
        ctx.fill();
      }

      // Hot highlights
      for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i += 23) {
        const it = items[i];
        ctx.globalAlpha = it.a * 0.6 * structureLevel;
        ctx.fillStyle = hotCol;
        ctx.beginPath();
        ctx.arc(it.sx, it.sy, it.r * 0.65, 0, TAU);
        ctx.fill();
      }

      // Store projected positions
      if (!prevScreen || prevScreen.length !== N_PARTICLES * 2) {
        prevScreen = new Float32Array(N_PARTICLES * 2);
      }
      for (let i = 0; i < N_PARTICLES; i++) {
        const p = project(pos[i*3], pos[i*3+1], pos[i*3+2], cosY, sinY, cosT, sinT, cx, cy);
        prevScreen[i*2] = p.sx;
        prevScreen[i*2+1] = p.sy;
      }

      ctx.globalAlpha = 1;
      if (!disposed) animationId = requestAnimationFrame(draw);
    };

    resize();
    window.addEventListener("resize", debouncedResize);

    setupJax()
      .then(() => { if (!disposed) animationId = requestAnimationFrame(draw); })
      .catch((err) => {
        // fallback...
      });

    return () => {
      disposed = true;
      window.removeEventListener("resize", debouncedResize);
      if (resizeTimer) clearTimeout(resizeTimer);
      cancelAnimationFrame(animationId);
      try { targetArr?.dispose(); noiseArr?.dispose(); key?.dispose(); } catch {}
    };
  }, []);

  return <canvas ref={canvasRef} aria-hidden="true" className="pointer-events-none fixed inset-x-0 top-0 z-0 block" />;
}

function easeInOutCubic(t: number): number {
  return t < 0.5 ? 4 * t * t * t : 1 - Math.pow(-2 * t + 2, 3) / 2;
}
```
